I remember in January 2020, a co-worker of mine who had family in China was talking about how crazy it was getting over there, and how he had to keep sending them masks because there weren't any. I barely paid him any mind--Kobe had just died, and I was busy with my own problems. Week after week, my co-worker seemed to get more hysterical, talking about people dying, hospitals being built in record time, and I placated him--Dr. Drew on TV said the virus was no big deal! Just a cold! Then things started going missing in the office--hand sanitizer was being stolen, and paper towels and toilet paper. I had to start locking my desk. This was late February. I used to wake up at 4AM to get to work around 5:20AM so I could write and enjoy the silence, and I remember one morning I went to the supermarket at 5AM like I sometimes did, and the parking lot was full of cars--I had never seen this before. I ended up waiting in line almost fifty minutes to buy a chicken pot pie. Ten days later, it was all over--Covid was everywhere, the supermarkets were barren, and my city felt like a graveyard. The last time I stepped foot in my office, the security guard I was friendly with had bought up all the food in the company's mini store, and was running a black market out of his trunk. I bought a six pack of Perrier off of him for $20 because I love seltzer water and I was desperate. I was disturbed by this opportunistic change in his personality, and that was the last time I ever saw him--I've been working from home ever since. I've been fairly luck with Covid--I didn't lose anybody close (knock on wood), but my mom got it. Thankfully, she was vaccinated--could've been a lot worse. My best friend, on the other hand, lost his father last year, and two of my co-workers nearly died from it--one of them hasn't been right since. I learned a lot about people during Covid--people I thought I knew, I didn't really know. It brought out the best in some, and the worst in others. Maybe that was the only real good thing about it--you got to see who you really were.

One thing that's often overlooked in these discourses is that, historically, one doesn't want a populous that feels they have nothing left to lose. Because they'll eventually turn their attention towards the people with the disproportionate amount of wealth and power. And both of those things only hold value so long as people believe in them.

Assuring worker's rights, fair pay, and quality of life isn't solely for the sake of doing the right thing... It's also for the sake of self-preservation.

Imagine the millions saved if an entire executive team is replaced by AI. If you think about it, all executives are really payed to do is sit around and think strategically, what does this better than AI?

It is often said that "People need to work in order to eat"
But I've read in a novel somewhere that "People need to eat in order to work"

It may seem the same, but the emphasis is not.

Automation and AI cut costs for companies but robots do not pay taxes or buy groceries. That makes it a self-eating snake. If too many people lose jobs the consumer base disappears and the whole system collapses. The fix is simple. Fine, use AI, but make them pay. Put a payroll-style tax on companies that replace workers so they still contribute as if those jobs existed. Tax the massive resources these data centers consume, from electricity to water that is taken away from local communities. Use that revenue to fund retraining, income credits, and lower household bills so people keep buying power.

Some will say we need to do whatever it takes to beat China or any other country in AI. But if beating them means hollowing out your own people, draining your towns for server farms, and leaving families broke, that is not victory, it is suicide. If China forsakes its own people for AI and robots it collapses too. Strength comes from citizens who can afford homes, raise families, and see a future, not from machines that never spend a dollar.

The biggest bang for the buck… err how to “maximize shareholder value” would be to replace the entire executive board at any major company with AI.

_"Who's going to buy everything.?"_

Nobody will buy anything. They didn't think that part through. Billionaires think that if they replace all their staff with AI, they can make even more money. That they can just sit around, push a button and watch the money roll in. This shows a basic lack of understanding of economics. It also shows that we as a society need to get over our child-like worship of billionaires.
Mass unemployment, poor food security and desperate people who feel like they have nothing left to lose is how governments are toppled and society radically transformed.

Of all of the dystopian fiction I’ve read, Margaret Atwood’s MaddAddam trilogy really seems like the most accurate to how things will actually be. A world separated into walled off rich corporate world and then the rest of us, the pleebs.

It tells the story of how we massively use resources around the world for our short term profit, by deforestation, mining, burning fossil fuels, consuming and expanding. This sadly leads to the many environmental issues we face today, including global pollution, climate change, and the extinction of animal species.

The documentary started by the image of Earth was full of plants, and then rivers, jungles, the oceans, all beautiful creatures like bear, sea lions, birds, etc, then suddenly, everything turned to another image: human and industrialization. 
Our planet was not full with plants, trees, or animal anymore, we destroyed forests to take wood, to crop. We built buildings, factories instead of plants and trees, we littered everywhere, trash, plastic bags, and plastic bottles were piled up like mountains, explosion of human population, resources are being exploited, the ecosystem is being changed and we can never predict what will come for us. In the near future, I believe, if this situation keeps on happening, we will experience “the age of Wall-e”, it means our Earth will become the biggest landfills of the whole universe. And what about us? Well, I can only say that we may face with the risk of extinction cause we still not have the giant spaceship and we still need air to breath (say no to live out-of-space,please). Everything can happen if we do not protect our home cause we ONLY have one home: EARTH.
